---
title: "Integrate with Harmony or scVI"
description: "Batch-correct and cluster across samples."
categories: [openpipeline]
order: 30
---

[`openpipeline`]{.pkg-tag .pkg-openpipeline}<span class="swatch openpipeline"></span>

Integration is a **separate workflow** run after
[`process_samples`](process-filter.qmd). It takes the processed object as input
and produces a batch-corrected embedding, Leiden clusters, and a UMAP.

:::{.callout-important}
Integration is not part of `process_samples`. Run it independently once per
cohort, using the `process_samples` output as its input.
:::

## Why batch correction

Samples from different donors, sequencing runs, or library preparations will
cluster by technical origin rather than biology if left uncorrected. Batch
correction aligns cell-type clusters across batches without erasing real
biological variation.

## The dimensionality reduction chain

Every integration workflow runs the same fixed sequence on top of the PCA
produced by `process_samples`:

1. **Batch correction** — adjusts the embedding so cells of the same identity
   from different batches overlap. Harmony operates in PCA space; scVI and
   totalVI learn a corrected latent space with a variational autoencoder.
2. **Neighbor graph** — builds a k-nearest-neighbor graph in the corrected space.
3. **Leiden clustering** — community detection on the graph. `--leiden_resolution`
   controls granularity: higher values give more, smaller clusters.
4. **UMAP** — projects the graph to 2D for visualization. Local structure is
   preserved; distances between clusters are not quantitatively meaningful.

The corrected embedding (`.obsm["X_pca_integrated"]`) is what downstream tools
use — not the UMAP coordinates.

## Choose a method

| Workflow | Method | When to use |
|---|---|---|
| `integration/harmony_leiden` | Harmony | Fast, good default for most datasets |
| `integration/scvi_leiden` | scVI | Deep learning; complex batch structure, large datasets |
| `integration/scanorama_leiden` | Scanorama | Partial cell-type overlap across batches |
| `integration/bbknn_leiden` | BBKNN | Batch-balanced neighbor graph; no corrected embedding |
| `integration/totalvi_leiden` | totalVI | Multiomics (RNA + ADT / CITE-seq); corrects both jointly |

A practical guide:

- Start with **Harmony** — fast, interpretable, works well for most datasets.
- Use **scVI** with many samples (>20), strong batch effects, or when you need a
  probabilistic latent space downstream.
- Use **totalVI** for matched RNA + ADT (CITE-seq).
- Use **Scanorama** when batches have different cell-type compositions.
- **BBKNN** modifies only the neighbor graph — lighter, but no corrected
  embedding for downstream use.

## Run Harmony (recommended starting point)

```bash
nextflow run openpipelines-bio/openpipeline \
  -r 4.2.0 -latest -profile docker \
  -main-script target/nextflow/workflows/integration/harmony_leiden/main.nf \
  --id cohort_01 \
  --input cohort_01_multisample.h5mu \
  --obs_covariates "sample_id" \
  --leiden_resolution 1.0 \
  --output cohort_01_integrated.h5mu \
  --publish_dir results/
```

:::{.callout-note}
`--obs_covariates` must name an existing `.obs` column that identifies the
technical source of variation — typically sample ID, batch, or donor.
:::

## Run scVI (large or complex datasets)

```bash
nextflow run openpipelines-bio/openpipeline \
  -r 4.2.0 -latest -profile docker \
  -main-script target/nextflow/workflows/integration/scvi_leiden/main.nf \
  --id cohort_01 \
  --input cohort_01_multisample.h5mu \
  --obs_batch "sample_id" \
  --leiden_resolution 1.0 \
  --output cohort_01_integrated.h5mu \
  --publish_dir results/
```

## Output

| Slot | Content |
|---|---|
| `.obsm["X_pca_integrated"]` | Batch-corrected cell embedding |
| `.obs["leiden"]` | Leiden cluster labels |
| `.obsm["X_umap"]` | UMAP coordinates for visualization |
| `.obsp["distances"]` / `.obsp["connectivities"]` | Neighbor graph |

Clusters are numbered — they have no biological meaning yet. Assign labels next
with [cell type annotation](annotate.qmd).

**Next:** [Annotate cell types →](annotate.qmd) ·
[every parameter →](../reference/index.qmd)
